## NEURAL STRUCTURED PREDICTION FOR INDUCTIVE NODE CLASSIFICATION

1Mila - Qu´ebec AI Institute

2Universit´e de Montr´eal

3HEC Montr´eal

4Canadian Institute for Advanced Research (CIFAR)

## ABSTRACT

This paper studies node classification in the inductive setting, i.e., aiming to learn a model on labeled training graphs and generalize it to infer node labels on unlabeled test graphs. This problem has been extensively studied with graph neural networks (GNNs) by learning effective node representations, as well as traditional structured prediction methods for modeling the structured output of node labels, e.g., conditional random fields (CRFs). In this paper, we present a new approach called the Structured Proxy Network (SPN), which combines the advantages of both worlds. SPN defines flexible potential functions of CRFs with GNNs. However, learning such a model is nontrivial as it involves optimizing a maximin game with high-cost inference. Inspired by the underlying connection between joint and marginal distributions defined by Markov networks, we propose to solve an approximate version of the optimization problem as a proxy, which yields a nearoptimal solution, making learning more efficient. Extensive experiments on two settings show that our approach outperforms many competitive baselines[1] .

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Graph-structured data are ubiquitous in the real world, covering a variety of applications. This paper studies node classification, a fundamental problem in the machine learning community. Most existing efforts focus on the transductive setting (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Velickoviˇ c et al., 2018), i.e., using´ a small set of labeled nodes in a graph to classify the rest of nodes. In this paper, we study node classification in the inductive setting (Hamilton et al., 2017), which is receiving growing interest. Given some training graphs with all nodes labeled, we aim to classify nodes in unlabeled test graphs.

This problem has been recently studied with graph neural networks (GNNs) (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017; Velickoviˇ c et al., 2018).´ GNNs infer the marginal label distribution of each node by learning useful node representations based on node features and edges. Once a GNN is learned on training graphs, it can be further applied to test graphs to infer node labels. Owing to the high capacity of nonlinear neural architectures, GNNs achieve impressive results on many datasets. However, one limitation of GNNs is that they ignore the joint dependency of node labels, and therefore node labels are predicted separately without modeling structured output.

Indeed, modeling structured output has been widely explored by the literature of structured prediction (BakIr et al., 2007). Structured prediction methods predict node labels collectively, so the label prediction of each node can be improved according to the predicted labels of neighboring nodes. One representative approach is the conditional random field (CRF) (Lafferty et al., 2001). A CRF models the joint distribution of node labels with Markov networks, and thus training CRFs becomes a learning task in graphical models, while predicting node labels corresponds to an inference task. Typically, the potential functions in CRFs are parameterized as log-linear functions, which suffer from low model capacities. One remedy for this is to define potential functions with GNNs (Ma et al., 2018; Qu et al., 2019). However, most of the effective methods for learning CRFs involve a

maximin game (Wainwright & Jordan, 2008; Sutton & McCallum, 2012), making learning often hard to converge, especially when GNNs are used to parameterize potential functions. Besides, as learning CRFs requires doing inference on the graphical models, the combined model requires a long run time.

In this paper, we address these challenges by proposing SPN (Structured Proxy Network), which is high in capacity, efficient in learning, and able to model the joint dependency of node labels. SPN is inspired by theoretical works in graphical models (Wainwright & Jordan, 2008), which reveal close connections between the joint label distribution and the node/edge marginal label distribution in a Markov network. Based on that, we approximate the original optimization problem with a proxy problem, where the potential functions in CRFs are defined by combining a collection of node/edge pseudomarginal distributions, which are parameterized by GNNs that satisfy a few simple constraints. This proxy problem can be easily solved by maximizing the data likelihood on each node and edge, which yields a near-optimal joint label distribution on training graphs. Once the model is learned, we apply it to test graphs and run loopy belief propagation (Murphy et al., 1999) to infer node labels. Experiments on two settings against both GNNs and CRFs prove the effectiveness of our approach.

Note that although SPN is tested on inductive node classification, this method is quite general and can be applied to many other structured prediction tasks as well, such as POS tagging (Church, 1988) and named entity recognition (Sang & De Meulder, 2003). Please refer to Sec. 4.3 for more details.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Graph neural networks (GNNs) perform node classification by learning useful node representations (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017; Velickoviˇ c et al., 2018).´ Most earlier efforts focus on designing GNNs for transductive node classification (Yang et al., 2016; Gao & Ji, 2019; Xhonneux et al., 2020), and many recent works move to the inductive setting (Hamilton et al., 2017; Gao et al., 2018; Chiang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020a; Zeng et al., 2020). Because of high capacity and efficient training, GNNs achieve impressive results on inductive node classification. Despite the success, GNNs only try to model the marginal distribution of each node label and predict node labels separately without considering joint dependency. In contrast, SPN models joint distributions of node labels with CRFs, which predicts node labels collectively to improve results.

Another type of approach for inductive node classification is structured prediction, which focuses on modeling the dependency of node labels, so that the predicted node labels are more consistent. One representative approach is structured SVM (Tsochantaridis et al., 2005; Finley & Joachims, 2008; Sarawagi & Gupta, 2008), but it lacks a probabilistic interpretation to handle the uncertainty of the prediction. Another representative probabilistic approach is conditional random field (Lafferty et al., 2001; Sutton & McCallum, 2006), which models the distribution of output spaces by using a Markov network. CRFs have been proven effective in many applications, such as POS tagging (Lafferty et al., 2001), shallow parsing (Sha & Pereira, 2003), image labeling (He et al., 2004), and sequence labeling (Lample et al., 2016; Ma & Hovy, 2016; Liu et al., 2018). Nevertheless, the potential functions in CRFs are typically defined as log-linear functions, suffering from low model capacity.

There are also some recent works trying to combine GNNs and CRFs. Some works use GNNs to solve inference problems in graphical models (Dai et al., 2016; Satorras et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020b; Satorras & Welling, 2020). In contrast, our approach uses GNNs to parameterize the potential functions in CRFs, which is in a similar vein to Ma et al. (2018); Qu et al. (2019); Ma et al. (2019; 2021); Wang et al. (2021). Among them, Ma et al. (2018) and Qu et al. (2019) optimize the pseudolikelihood (Besag, 1975) for model learning, and Wang et al. (2021) optimizes a cross-entropy loss on each single node, which can yield poor approximation of the true joint likelihood (Koller & Friedman, 2009; Sutton & McCallum, 2012). Our approach instead solves a proxy problem, which yields a near-optimal solution to the original problem of maximizing likelihood, and thus gets superior results. For Ma et al. (2019) and Ma et al. (2021), they focus on transductive node classification and continuous labels respectively, which are different from our work.

Lastly, learning CRFs has also been widely studied. Some works solve a maximin game as a surrogate for learning (Sutton & McCallum, 2012) and some others maximize a lower bound of the likelihood function (Sutton & McCallum, 2009). However, these maximin games are often hard to optimize and the lower bounds are often loose. Different from them, we follow Wainwright et al. (2003) and build an approximate optimization problem as a proxy, which is easier to solve and yields better results.

This paper focuses on inductive node classification (Hamilton et al., 2017), a fundamental problem in both graph machine learning and structured prediction. We employ a probabilistic formalization for the problem with some labeled training graphs and unlabeled test graphs. Each training graph is given as ( **y** _V[∗][,]_ **[ x]** _[V][ , E]_[)][, where] **[ x]** _[V]_[and] **[ y]** _V[∗]_[are features and labels of a set of nodes] _[ V]_[ , and] _[ E]_[is a set of] edges. For each test graph ( **x** _V_ ˜ _, E_[˜] ), only features **x** _V_ ˜ and edges _E_[˜] are given. Then we aim to solve:

• **Learning.** On training graphs, learn a probabilistic model to approximate _p_ ( **y** _V |_ **x** _V , E_ ). • **Inference.** For each test graph, infer node labels **y** _V[∗]_ ˜[according to the distribution] _[ p]_[(] **[y]** _V_[ ˜] _[|]_ **[x]** _V_[ ˜] _[,][E]_[˜][)][.]
The problem has been extensively studied in both graph machine learning and structured prediction fields, and representative methods are GNNs and CRFs respectively. Next, we introduce the details.

## 3.1 GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS

For inductive node classification, graph neural networks (GNNs) learn node representations to predict marginal label distributions of nodes. GNNs assume all node labels are independent conditioned on node features and edges, so the joint label distribution is factorized into a set of marginals as below:

Each marginal distribution _pθ_ ( _ys|_ **x** _V , E_ ) is modeled as a categorical distribution over label candidates, and the label probabilities are computed by applying a linear softmax classifier to the representation of node _s_ . In general, node representations are learned via the message passing mechanism (Gilmer et al., 2017), which brings high capacity to GNNs. Also, owing to the factorization in Eq. (1), learning and inference can be easily solved in GNNs, where we simply need to compute loss and make prediction on each node separately. However, GNNs approximate only the marginal label distributions of nodes on training graphs, which may generalize badly and result in poor approximation of node marginal label distributions on test graphs. Also, the labels of different nodes are separately predicted according to their own marginal label distributions, yet the joint dependency of node labels is ignored.

The major challenge of CRFs lies in learning. On the one hand, learning relies on inference, meaning that we have to update _{qs_ ( _ys_ ) _}s∈V_ , _{qst_ ( _ys, yt_ ) _}_ ( _s,t_ ) _∈E_ to approximate the node and edge marginals of _pθ_ at each step, which can be expensive. On the other hand, as learning involves a maximin game and the optimal _q_ of the inner minimization problem in Eq. (3) is intractable, we can only maximize an upper bound of the likelihood function for _θ_ , making learning unstable. The problem becomes even more severe when _θ_ is parameterized by highly nonlinear neural models, e.g. GNNs.

## 4 MODEL

In this section, we introduce our proposed approach Structured Proxy Network (SPN). The general idea of SPN is to combine GNNs and CRFs by parameterizing potential functions in CRFs with GNNs, and therefore SPN enjoys high capacity and can model the joint dependency of node labels.

However, as elaborated in Sec. 3.2, learning such a model on training graphs is challenging due to the maximin game in optimization. Inspired by the connection between the joint and marginal distributions of CRFs, we instead construct a new optimization problem, which serves as a proxy for model learning. Compared with the original maximin game, the proxy problem is much easier to solve, where we can simply train two GNNs to approximate the marginal label distributions on nodes and edges, and further combine these pseudomarginals (defined in Prop. 1) into a near-optimal joint label distribution. This joint label distribution can be further refined by optimizing the maximin game, although it is optional and often unnecessary, as this distribution is often close enough to the optimal one. With this proxy problem for model learning, learning becomes more stable and efficient.
Afterwards, the learned model is used to predict the joint label distribution on test graphs. Then we run loopy belief propagation to infer node labels. Now, we introduce the details of our approach.

## 4.1 LEARNING

The learning task aims at training _θ_ to maximize the log-likelihood function log _pθ_ ( **y** _V[∗][|]_ **[x]** _[V][ , E]_[)] for each training graph ( **y** _V[∗][,]_ **[ x]** _[V][ , E]_[)][,][which][is][highly][challenging.][Therefore,][instead][of][directly] optimizing this goal, we solve an approximate version of the problem as a proxy, which is training a node GNN and an edge GNN to maximize the log-likelihood of observed labels on nodes and edges.

## 4.3 DISCUSSION

In practice, many structured prediction problems can be viewed as special cases of inductive node classification, where the graphs between nodes have some special structures. For example in sequence labeling tasks (e.g., named entity recognition), the graphs between nodes have sequential structures. Thus, SPN can be applied to these tasks as well. In order for better results, one might replace GNNs with other neural models which are specifically designed for the studied task to better estimate the pseudomarginals. For example in sequence labeling tasks, recurrent neural networks can be used.

## 5 EXPERIMENT

## 5.1 DATASETS

We consider datasets in two settings, which focus on node-level and graph-level accuracy respectively.

**Node-level Accuracy.** The node-level accuracy measures _how likely a model can predict the correct label of a node in test graphs_ . We use the **PPI** dataset (Zitnik & Leskovec, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017), which has 20 training graphs. To make the dataset more challenging, we also try using only the first 1/2/10 training graphs, yielding another three datasets **PPI-1** , **PPI-2** , and **PPI-10** . Besides, we also build a **DBLP** dataset from the citation network in Tang et al. (2008). Papers from eight conferences are treated as nodes, and we split them into three categories for classification according to conference domains[2] . For each paper, we compute the mean GloVe embedding (Pennington et al., 2014) of words in the title and abstract as node features. The training/validation/test graph is formed as the citation graph of papers published before 1999, from 2000 to 2009, after 2010 respectively.

**Graph-level Accuracy.** The graph-level accuracy measures _how likely a model can correctly classify all the nodes for a given test graph_ . We construct three datasets from the Cora, Citeseer, and Pubmed datasets used for transductive node classification (Yang et al., 2016). Each raw dataset has a single graph. For each training/validation/test node of the raw dataset, we treat its ego network[3] as a training/validation/test graph. We denote the datasets as **Cora*** , **Citeseer*** , **Pubmed*** .

## 5.2 COMPARED ALGORITHMS

**Graph Neural Networks.** For GNNs, we choose a few well-known model architectures for comparison, including GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017), GraphSage (Hamilton et al., 2017), GAT (Velickoviˇ c´ et al., 2018), Graph U-Net (Gao & Ji, 2019) and GCNII (Chen et al., 2020a).

**Conditional Random Fields.** For CRFs, we consider three variants. (1) _CRF-linear._ This variant uses linear _θ_ -functions in Eq. (2), which takes the features on nodes and edges for computation. (2) _CRF-GNN._ This variant parameterizes the _θ_ -functions as _θs_ ( _ys_ ) = _f_ ( **u** _s_ ) and _θst_ ( _ys, yt_ ) = _g_ ( **v** _s,_ **v** _t_ ), with _f_ and _g_ defined in Eq. (7) and Eq. (8), where the node representations are generated by different GNN architectures (e.g., CRF-GAT). We train these models via the maximin game as in Eq. (3) with sum-product loopy belief propagation. (3) _GMNN._ We also consider GMNN (Qu et al., 2019), an approach combining GNNs and CRFs, which optimizes the pseudolikelihood function for learning.

**Our Approach.** For SPNs, we try different GNN architectures for defining the node and edge GNNs (e.g., SPN-GAT). By default, we only solve the proxy problem without performing refinement. We systematically compare the results with and without refinement in part 2 of Sec. 5.5.

## 5.3 EVALUATION METRICS

On Cora*, Citeseer*, and Pubmed*, we report the percentage of test graphs where all the nodes are correctly classified (i.e., graph-level accuracy). On DBLP and PPI, we report accuracy and micro-F1 based on the percentage of test nodes which are correctly classified (i.e., node-level accuracy). For Cora*, Citeseer*, and Pubmed*, we run each compared method with 10 different seeds to report the mean accuracy and the standard deviation. For DBLP and PPI, we run each method with 5 seeds.

## 5.4 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

For GNNs, by default we use the same architectures (e.g., number of neurons, number of layers) as used in the original papers. Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2015) is used for training. For the edge GNN in Eq. (8), we add a hyperparameter _γ_ to control the annealing temperature of the logit _g_ ( **v** _s,_ **v** _t_ ) before the softmax function during belief propagation. Empirically, we find that max-product belief propagation works better than the sum-product variant in most cases, so we use the max-product version by default. By default, we do not run refinement when training SPNs. See Sec. F for details.

## 5.5 RESULTS

**1. Comparison with other methods.** The main results in the two settings are presented in Tab. 1 and Tab. 2. Compared against different GNN models, our approach achieves consistent improvement (the relative underperformance of SPN-GCN and SPN-SAGE is related to the capacity of the backbone GNNs and is explained in Sec. G.1) by using these GNNs as backbone networks for approximating marginal label distributions on nodes and edges, which demonstrates SPNs are able to model the structured output of node labels by combining with CRFs, and thus achieve better results.

Besides, SPNs also achieve superior results to CRF-GNNs which are trained by directly solving the maximin game in Eq. (3), as well as GMNN which optimizes the pseudolikelihood function. This observation proves the advantage of our proposed proxy optimization problem for learning CRFs.

**2. Effect of refinement.** By solving the proxy optimization problem in Eq. (9), we can obtain a near-optimal joint label distribution on training graphs, based on which we may optionally refine the distribution with the maximin game in Eq. (3). Next, we study the effect of refinement, and we present the results in Tab. 4. By only solving the proxy problem, our approach already achieves impressive results, showing that the proxy problem can well approximate the original learning problem. Only on datasets with sufficient labeled data (e.g., PPI-10, PPI), refinement leads to some improvement.

**3. Model architecture.** SPN uses a node GNN and an edge GNN for computing node and edge marginals independently. In practice, we can also use a shared GNN for both node and edge marginals. We show results of this variant in Tab. 6, where it also achieves significant improvement over GNNs.
**4. Efficiency comparison.** We have seen SPNs achieve better classification results than GNNs and CRFs. Next, we further compare their efficiency by showing the run time on DBLP and PPI. For PPI,

**5. Comparison of learning methods.** Next, we investigate different methods for learning SPNs, including directly solving the maximin game, optimizing pseudolikelihood, and solving our proposed proxy problem. We show the results for optimizing SPN-GAT in Tab. 5. We see solving maximin game yields poor results due to unstable training. Although the pseudolikelihood method performs much better, the result is still unsatisfactory as it is not a good approximation of the true likelihood. By solving our proposed proxy problem, SPN achieves the best result, which proves its effectiveness.

**6. Convergence analysis.** To better illustrate the advantage of the proxy problem for learning CRFs, we look into the training curves of SPNs, SPNs w/o proxy, and CRFs when optimizing the maximin game in Eq. (3). For SPNs, we optimize the node and edge GNNs on the proxy optimization problem in Eq. (9) before doing refinement with the maximin game, while for SPNs w/o proxy we directly perform refinement with the maximin game without solving the proxy problem. We show the results in Fig. 2. CRFs and SPNs w/o proxy suffer from high variance and low accuracy. In contrast, owing to the near-optimal joint distribution found by solving the proxy problem, SPNs get much higher accuracy with lower variance even without refinement (see initial results of SPNs at epoch 0). Also, the refinement process quickly converges after only a few epochs, showing good efficiency of SPNs.

**7. Case study.** To intuitively see how SPNs outperform GNNs, we conduct some case studies on Cora*. We use GAT as backbone networks, and show the prediction made by the GAT (the node GNN), the edge GNN, and SPN in Fig. 3. In all three cases shown in the figure, GAT (left column) makes inconsistent predictions on linked nodes, as it fails to model the structured output. The edge GNN (middle column) also makes a mistake in the bottom case. Finally, by combining GAT and edge GNN with a CRF, the SPN (right column) is able to predict the correct labels for all nodes.

## 6 CONCLUSION

This paper studied inductive node classification, and we proposed SPN to combine GNNs and CRFs. Inspired by the connection of joint and marginal distributions defined by Markov networks, we designed a proxy problem for efficient model learning. In the future, we plan to explore more advanced GNNs to model the pseudomarginals on edges, which are key to improving node classification results in SPNs. In addition, SPNs model joint dependency of node labels by defining potential functions on nodes and edges, and we also plan to further explore high-order local structures, e.g., triangles.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to inductive node classification by integrating the strengths of graph neural networks (GNNs) and structured prediction through the proposed Structured Proxy Network (SPN). The writing is clear and logically structured, with a strong narrative that effectively frames the limitations of existing methods—GNNs' disregard for label dependencies and traditional CRFs' limited expressiveness—and positions SPN as a principled solution bridging these paradigms. The key insight of leveraging pseudomarginal distributions parameterized by GNNs to approximate the otherwise intractable CRF learning problem is both novel and insightful, with theoretical grounding in graphical models. However, the most significant issue lies in the somewhat underspecified technical treatment of the proxy optimization: while the high-level idea is compelling, the paper would benefit from greater clarity on how exactly the pseudomarginal constraints are enforced in practice and how closely the resulting approximation matches the true joint distribution. Additionally, although the method claims efficiency gains over standard CRF learning, a more detailed runtime or convergence analysis would strengthen the argument. The empirical evaluation appears promising but could be expanded to include ablation studies that isolate the contribution of structured modeling versus representation learning. Overall, the paper makes a meaningful contribution to structured graph prediction with a conceptually elegant design, though minor improvements in exposition and technical detail would elevate its impact.

comments.title: Severe OCR Errors in Mathematical Notation Undermining Formal Clarity
comments.explanation: Multiple OCR artifacts severely corrupt key mathematical expressions throughout the paper, most critically in the pseudomarginal consistency constraints (Proposition 1, Eq. 9) where summation symbols (∑) are replaced with unreadable characters (e.g., '_'). The intended constraints—∑_{ys} τ_st(ys, yt) = τ_t(yt) and ∑_{yt} τ_st(ys, yt) = τ_s(ys)—are standard in variational inference (e.g., Wainwright & Jordan, 2008), but their formal rendering is compromised. The issue persists in Eq. (4), where I_{(ys∗,yt∗)} is truncated to I_{ys∗}, and in later derivations, propagating confusion. Although the prose and context suggest the correct meaning, these artifacts impair accessibility for readers unfamiliar with moment matching and erode confidence in the technical content. Unlike isolated typos, this is a systemic rendering failure affecting core formalism.

comments.title: Misstatement of CRF Moment-Matching Conditions
comments.explanation: Equation (4) incorrectly states the maximum-likelihood moment-matching conditions for CRFs. It claims that the model marginals pθ(ys) and pθ(ys,yt) equal delta functions I_{ys∗}(ys) and I_{(ys∗,yt∗)}(ys,yt), implying full distributional equality. The correct condition is that the *expected* sufficient statistics match empirical ones: E_{pθ}[ys] = ys* and E_{pθ}[ys,yt] = (ys*,yt*). This distinction is crucial: moment matching does not require the model marginals to be degenerate, only that their expectations align with the data. Stating distributional equality misrepresents CRF theory and could mislead readers about the nature of optimality. The paper's algorithmic intent—training pseudomarginals to approximate delta functions—effectively matches sufficient statistics under one-hot encoding, so the practical method is sound. However, the theoretical framing is formally incorrect and should be revised to reflect that expected values (not distributions) match.

comments.title: Incorrect Direction of Variational Bound in CRF Learning
comments.quote: we can only maximize an upper bound of the likelihood function for θ
comments.explanation: The paper claims that intractable CRF learning requires maximizing an 'upper bound' of the likelihood function, which is conceptually incorrect. In variational inference, the evidence lower bound (ELBO) is maximized—a *lower* bound on the log-likelihood. The dual formulation involves a maximin game where the inner minimization produces an upper bound on the negative log-likelihood (or free energy), but the overall objective remains maximization of a lower bound on the likelihood. Misstating this as 'maximize an upper bound' risks confusing readers about the fundamental mechanics of variational methods, especially those new to graphical models. The standard reference (Wainwright & Jordan, 2008) clearly treats this as lower-bound maximization. While the intended meaning may be inferable by experts, the phrasing misaligns with established terminology and constitutes a conceptual error in exposition.

comments.title: Underspecification of GNN Parameter Updates in Refinement Step
comments.quote: we update the θ-functions parameterized by the node and edge GNNs to maximize:
comments.explanation: The refinement step is ambiguously described: it states that θ-functions (defined via Eq. 5 as deterministic functions of pseudomarginals τs and τst, which are outputs of the node and edge GNNs) are updated to maximize an unspecified objective. Two critical issues arise: (1) The actual objective function is missing after the colon, despite reference to positive/negative examples in the following intuition; (2) It's unclear whether the GNN parameters are being fine-tuned (thus changing τs and τst) or whether θ is updated with separate parameters. If the GNNs are updated, it breaks the theoretical link in Proposition 1, where τs and τst are trained independently to match empirical marginals. Without clarification, reproducibility is compromised. Even if the objective aligns with a standard contrastive or maximin update (e.g., expected score of true labels minus samples from q), the lack of formalism and implementation detail creates a methodological gap.

comments.title: Unjustified Assumption of Natural Pseudomarginal Consistency
comments.quote: the optimal pseudomarginals τ should well approximate the observed node and edge marginals... and hence τ will almost naturally satisfy the consistency constraint.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that training node and edge GNNs to independently fit empirical marginals (i.e., τs → δ_{ys*}, τst → δ_{(ys*,yt*)}) implies that pseudomarginals 'almost naturally' satisfy the consistency constraints ∑_{ys} τst(ys, yt) = τt(yt). However, this is not guaranteed. While empirical marginals are consistent, separate training of τs and τst through different GNNs with independent objectives provides no formal mechanism to ensure consistency of predictions. The paper offers no empirical validation, theoretical argument, or architectural coupling (e.g., shared representations) to justify this assumption. It further complicates matters by noting that θ-functions derived from τs and τst may be inconsistent if GNNs are fine-tuned later. The claim is thus an unjustified overstatement, relying on 'empirical closeness to delta functions' without showing that this suffices for approximate consistency. This constitutes a methodological weakness in the theoretical motivation.

comments.title: Ambiguous Edge GNN Architecture and Representation Flow
comments.quote: Similarly, we apply the edge GNN to compute a representation **v** _s_ for each node _s_, and model _τst_ ( _ys, yt_ ) as:
comments.explanation: The paper states that the edge GNN computes a representation **v**_s_ 'for each node _s_', yet τst(ys, yt) is a pairwise function requiring information from both _s_ and _t_. It is unclear how per-node representations from an 'edge GNN' are used to define a joint distribution. The text does not specify whether the edge GNN outputs per-edge representations, or whether **v**_s_ and **v**_t_ are computed and then combined (e.g., concatenated) as input to function _g_. While Eq. (7) and Figure 1 suggest the latter, the prose contradicts this by suggesting node-local computation. This creates ambiguity in reproduction: a reader may assume **v**_s_ is node-only and fail to incorporate edge-specific or pairwise context. The lack of input clarification (e.g., edge features, message-passing mechanism) compounds the issue. This architectural vagueness affects understanding of how relational knowledge is captured, a core contribution claim of the method.

comments.title: Missing Clarification on Shared GNN Variant Implementation
comments.explanation: Table 6 reports results for a variant where node and edge GNNs share parameters, outperforming the separate-GNN version on PPI-10. However, the implementation is underspecified: it is unclear how a single GNN produces both node-level and edge-level pseudomarginals. Are weights shared in the backbone with separate heads? Is the edge representation derived from paired node embeddings? How are edge features integrated? While the default method uses distinct GNNs, switching to a shared architecture is a substantive design change with implications for parameter efficiency, inductive bias, and relational modeling. Without explanation, the result lacks methodological transparency, and performance differences could stem from architectural confounds rather than principled advantages. This omission limits interpretability and reproducibility of a cited variant.

comments.title: Citation Misattribution Regarding Theoretical Foundation of Proxy Problem
comments.quote: Different from them, we follow Wainwright et al. (2003) and build an approximate optimization problem as a proxy...
comments.explanation: The paper cites Wainwright et al. (2003) as the foundation for its proxy optimization framework, but this is inaccurate. Wainwright et al. (2003) focuses on expectation propagation and convex duality for variational inference, not on constructing joint distributions via learned pseudomarginals with local consistency. The core idea—linking pseudomarginals to belief propagation fixed points and marginal polytope approximations—is more directly grounded in Wainwright & Jordan (2008), which is already cited elsewhere. While the authors demonstrate awareness of the correct theory, citing Wainwright et al. (2003) misattributes conceptual origins and may misdirect readers seeking foundational understanding. comments.title: Imprecise Claim About Near-Optimality of Proxy Solution
comments.quote: we propose to solve an approximate version of the optimization problem as a proxy, which yields a nearoptimal solution, making learning more efficient.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction assert that the proxy problem 'yields a nearoptimal solution' to the original maximum-likelihood learning problem. While Proposition 1 and the surrounding discussion provide intuition—linking pseudomarginals to fixed points of belief propagation—the paper does not offer a formal bound or condition under which the solution to the proxy problem (maximizing node and edge likelihoods) is guaranteed to be close to the true maximum-likelihood estimator. In graphical models, moment-matching under approximate inference (e.g., mean field or BP fixed points) does not generally imply closeness in parameter space or likelihood without strong assumptions (e.g., graph tree structure, weak coupling). The claim is plausible and empirically supported, but presented as a theoretical outcome rather than a heuristic with empirical validation. A more precise statement would be that the proxy 'encourages satisfaction of moment-matching conditions', not that it 'yields a nearoptimal solution'. This overstatement risks misleading readers about the theoretical strength of the contribution, though it does not break the method.

comments.title: Incorrect Citation of Wainwright et al. (2003) for Proxy Optimization Framework
comments.quote: Different from them, we follow Wainwright et al. (2003) and build an approximate optimization problem as a proxy, which is easier to solve and yields better results.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement seems reasonable: the authors position their proxy learning method in contrast to maximin games and variational lower bounds, citing Wainwright et al. (2003) as prior theoretical grounding for building tractable approximations via marginal consistency. However, Wainwright et al. (2003) — more accurately, the technical report 'Expectation Propagation for Exponential Families' (Wainwright, Jaakkola, Jordan, 2003) — primarily develops expectation propagation and variational approximations using convex duality and entropy bounds, focusing on mean-field and tree-reweighted relaxations. The core idea in SPN — constructing a joint distribution via learned pseudomarginals satisfying local consistency constraints — aligns more closely with the moment-matching and belief propagation fixed-point results in Wainwright & Jordan (2008), which is already cited elsewhere. Wainwright et al. (2003) does not explicitly propose a 'proxy optimization' framework of the kind described here (i.e., maximum pseudomarginal likelihood under consistency conditions). The correct theoretical antecedent for combining marginal estimation with moment-matching in CRF learning appears to be Wainwright & Jordan (2008), particularly the variational formulation of likelihood maximization via marginal polytope approximations. While the authors clearly understand the relevant theory (as seen in Proposition 1), citing Wainwright et al. (2003) misattributes the conceptual origin of the proxy approach. This weakens the scholarly framing and may misdirect readers seeking foundations. The issue is not about technical correctness of SPN but the alignment of citation to methodological inspiration.

The authors later treat τs and τst as *learned approximations to the true marginals*, but Proposition 1 presents them as abstract distributions satisfying consistency. The ambiguity arises in whether these are constrained latent objects or fitted parameters. This is resolved partially in Section 4.1, where τs and τst are explicitly parameterized by GNNs and trained to fit ground truth. But within the proposition alone, their nature is underspecified. Moreover, the condition refers to (st) ∈ E but later uses (s,t), suggesting OCR inconsistency. However, this is trivially reconcilable. The core issue is pedagogical: a reader might initially think τs and τst are derived from some optimization (like in variational inference), rather than being *directly learned outputs*. While context later resolves this, clearer labeling — e.g., 'GNN-output pseudomarginals' — would reduce ambiguity.

comments.title: Inaccurate Reference to Moment-Matching Conditions in Equation (4)
comments.explanation: The moment-matching conditions in Eq. (4) claim that at optimality, the model marginals pθ(ys) and pθ(ys,yt) equal the indicator functions of the true labels ys* and (ys*,yt*). This is incorrect in general. In maximum likelihood learning of CRFs, the correct moment-matching condition is that the expected sufficient statistics under the model match those under the empirical distribution. For node and edge potentials, this means: E_pθ[ys] = ys* and E_pθ[ys,yt] = (ys*,yt*), not that the entire distribution collapses to a point mass. In other words, it's the expectations that match, not the full distributions. Stating pθ(ys) = I_{ys*}(ys) implies perfect certainty and degenerate marginals, which is only true in the limit of infinite data and perfect separation — not a general property of CRF optima. The correct condition is ∑_yV pθ(yV | xV, E) ys = ys* and similarly for edges, i.e., Epθ[ys] = ys*. The current formulation conflates distributional equality with moment matching. While in the case of binary or categorical one-hot labels, the expected label vector equals the indicator of the true label, the way it's written suggests the *distribution* is identical to the delta function, which is misleading. This misstatement could lead readers to misunderstand CRF optimality. However, the algorithmic use in SPN — encouraging τs ≈ I_{ys*} — suggests they are effectively matching sufficient statistics, so the *intent* aligns with correct theory even if the *expression* is imprecise.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of moment-matching conditions in final implication
comments.quote: Combining these two sets of formula results inconditions in Eq. (4) for the optimal joint label distribution are roughly achieved, implying that the _pθ_ ( _ys_ ) _≈_ I _ys∗ {ys}_ and _pθ_ ( _ys, yt_ ) _≈_ I _ys∗ {ys}_ .
comments.explanation: The text claims that satisfying the pseudomarginal approximations (τs ≈ I_{ys∗}, τst ≈ I_{(ys∗, yt∗)}) and using Eq. (5) implies pθ(ys) ≈ I_{ys∗} and pθ(ys, yt) ≈ I_{ys∗}. However, the final term is clearly a typo: 'I _ys∗ {ys}' appears again for the edge marginal, but it should be 'I_{(ys∗, yt∗)}{(ys, yt)}'. This is not just a notational slip — it fundamentally misstates what the moment-matching condition requires for edges. The edge marginal must match the joint indicator over pairs, not a node-wise one. While context suggests this is an OCR or typesetting error (the earlier uses of I_{(ys∗,yt∗)} are correct), the sentence as written incorrectly suggests that pθ(ys, yt) ≈ I_{ys∗}{ys}, which is ill-defined and does not align with Eq. (4). Even assuming the intended symbol was I_{(ys∗,yt∗)}, the omission of yt∗ breaks semantic correctness. This could mislead a reader into thinking edge matching reduces to node-level matching.

comments.title: Ambiguity in edge GNN representation computation
comments.quote: Similarly, we apply the edge GNN to compute a representation **v** _s_ for each node _s_ , and model _τst_ ( _ys, yt_ ) as:
comments.explanation: The sentence states that the edge GNN computes a representation **v**_s_ for each node _s_, but the edge pseudomarginal τst(ys, yt) depends on both nodes _s_ and _t_. It’s unclear how a per-node representation from an 'edge GNN' is used to define a pairwise distribution. Did the authors mean that the edge GNN computes a representation per edge (vs, vt), or per node, and then combines them? While Figure 1 shows EdgeGNN connected to edges, and Eq. (7) uses vs and vt, the prose says 'for each node s', which contradicts the apparent design. This creates ambiguity: is the edge GNN node-local or edge-local? A reader might reproduce the model incorrectly by computing per-node representations and failing to capture edge-specific context. The issue is resolved partially by Eq. (7), but the text misleads. Given that this is a core modeling component, the description should precisely reflect the parameterization.

comments.title: Incorrect direction of optimization bound in CRF learning discussion
comments.quote: On the other hand, as learning involves a maximin game and the optimal _q_ of the inner minimization problem in Eq. (3) is intractable, we can only maximize an upper bound of the likelihood function for _θ_ , making learning unstable.
comments.explanation: The passage claims that due to intractability, we 'can only maximize an upper bound of the likelihood function' for θ. This is incorrect. In variational inference for CRFs, the evidence lower bound (ELBO) is maximized — i.e., a lower bound on the log-likelihood — not an upper bound. The minimization over q in the maximin game yields log pθ(y∗_V | xV, E) = max_θ min_q { ⋯ }, and the inner min produces an upper bound on the negative log-likelihood, but the overall objective is to maximize a lower bound. Saying we maximize an 'upper bound' confuses the direction of optimization and the nature of the bound. While experienced readers may mentally correct this, it risks misleading those learning the topic. The standard formulation (e.g., Wainwright & Jordan, 2008) treats the dual as minimizing an upper bound on free energy, but the learning goal remains maximizing a lower bound on likelihood. This is a real conceptual error in framing.

comments.title: Misattribution of theoretical inspiration for proxy problem
comments.quote: The proxy problem is inspired by Wainwright & Jordan (2008), which points out that the marginal label distributions on nodes and edges defined by a Markov network have inherent connections with the joint distribution.
comments.explanation: The paper cites Wainwright & Jordan (2008) as the inspiration for the connection between marginals and joint distributions. However, in Section 2, the authors correctly cite Wainwright et al. (2003) as the source for building approximate optimization problems via variational principles. Wainwright & Jordan (2008) is a textbook that synthesizes material, while Wainwright et al. (2003) is the foundational work on variational inference and belief propagation approximations in graphical models. The actual construction in Proposition 1 — linking pseudomarginals to fixed points of belief propagation — is more directly grounded in Wainwright et al. (2003). Citing Wainwright & Jordan (2008) here is not wrong per se, but it misattributes the origin of the core theoretical idea. Given that the method explicitly references approximate optimization theory (Wainwright et al., 2003) in the abstract, the shift to (2008) here creates a mild inconsistency in credit attribution. This does not affect correctness but may confuse readers seeking the foundational reference.

comments.title: Objective for θ-update in refinement step is underspecified
comments.quote: In the second step, we update the θ-functions parameterized by the node and edge GNNs to maximize:
comments.explanation: The passage states that in the refinement step, the θ-functions are updated to maximize some objective, but it does not specify what that objective actually is. The sentence ends with a colon, suggesting that an equation should follow (likely involving expectations over true labels and samples from q), but no formula is provided in the given text. While it's plausible from context—such as the subsequent intuitive explanation involving positive and negative examples—that the intended objective is something like maximizing the expected score on ground-truth labels minus the expected score on samples from the variational distribution, the absence of a formal expression makes this update ambiguous. Given that this refinement is optional and not the core learning method, and the intuition is reasonably clear, the omission may be due to formatting or truncation. However, as written, a reader cannot reproduce the update rule. This is not resolved by the PAPER SUMMARY, which does not list the refinement objective.

comments.title: Inference of graph-level labels via max-product lacks decoding clarification
comments.quote: This problem can be approximately solved by the max-product variant of loopy belief propagation, which simply replaces the sum over yt ˜ in Eq. (12) with max (Weiss & Freeman, 2001). Afterwards, the best node label can be still decoded via Eq. (13).
comments.explanation: The paper claims that after applying max-product belief propagation, node labels can still be decoded using Eq. (13), which computes the marginal belief at each node via product of messages and node potential. However, in standard max-product (or max-sum) algorithms, the goal is to approximate the MAP configuration, and decoding often involves backtracking to recover the joint assignment that maximizes the distribution. While max-product messages can be used to approximate marginals, doing so without backtracking does not guarantee consistency across nodes and may not yield the argmax of the joint distribution. The paper does not clarify whether Eq. (13) is applied directly (as in sum-product) or modified for joint decoding. Given that graph-level accuracy depends on recovering the full correct label vector, this is a notable gap. The text may be oversimplifying by suggesting that marginal maximization (Eq. 13) suffices for recovering the full joint MAP assignment, which in general graphs is not valid due to lack of calibration in max-product beliefs. This could mislead a reader unfamiliar with the limitations of max-product.

comments.title: Ambiguity in refinement step’s use of GNN parameters
comments.quote: we update the θ-functions parameterized by the node and edge GNNs to maximize:
comments.explanation: The refinement step claims to update the θ-functions, which are defined in Eq. (5) as deterministic functions of the pseudomarginals τs and τst, which in turn are outputs of the NodeGNN and EdgeGNN. However, during refinement, it's unclear whether the GNNs themselves (i.e., their weights) are being updated to change θ, or whether θ is being updated directly. The pseudomarginals τ are trained only during the proxy phase, and Eq. (5) is a fixed transformation. If refinement updates the GNN parameters, then τ changes, but this would break the link between τ and the moment-matching objective unless τ continues to approximate the data marginals. The paper does not clarify whether the GNNs are fine-tuned during refinement or whether θ is updated directly (e.g., via separate parameters). This is a real methodological ambiguity: if the node and edge GNNs are updated, then their outputs τs and τst are no longer trained solely to fit empirical marginals, which undermines the theoretical justification in Proposition 1. The paper lacks detail on whether refinement modifies the GNNs or introduces separate θ parameters.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about consistency constraints emerging naturally
comments.quote: the optimal pseudomarginals τ should well approximate the observed node and edge marginals, i.e., τs(ys) ≈ Iys∗{ys} and τst(ys, yt) ≈ I(ys∗,yt∗){(ys, yt)}, and hence τ will almost naturally satisfy the consistency constraint.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that because the pseudomarginals are trained to fit empirical marginals (delta distributions at true labels), they will 'almost naturally' satisfy the consistency conditions ∑_ys τst(ys, yt) = τt(yt) and ∑_yt τst(ys, yt) = τs(ys). However, this is questionable: the empirical node marginals and edge marginals (from ground truth) do satisfy consistency, but the model is training τs and τst independently via separate GNNs to fit point masses. Since the node and edge GNNs are trained with independent objectives (minimizing KL to delta functions), there is no mechanism enforcing that their outputs are jointly consistent. In fact, unless the GNNs are explicitly regularized or constrained, their outputs may not satisfy ∑_ys τst(ys, yt) ≈ τt(yt), especially when predictions are noisy. The authors mention in the PAPER SUMMARY that 'optimization of the divergence objective naturally leads to their satisfaction'—but this lacks justification. A careful reader might expect empirical evidence or theoretical argument showing that independent fitting to consistent targets yields consistent predictions under their GNN parameterization, but none is provided. This is a mild overclaim, though it could hold approximately in practice with strong models.

comments.title: Potential confusion in notation for θ-functions in CRF-GNN
comments.quote: CRF-GNN. This variant parameterizes the θ-functions as θs(ys) = f(us) and θst(ys, yt) = g(vs, vt), with f and g defined in Eq. (7) and Eq. (8)
comments.explanation: In Eq. (7) and (8), f and g are functions used to define the pseudomarginals τs and τst via softmax, e.g., τs(ys) = softmax(f(us)). However, in CRF-GNN, θs(ys) is directly set to f(us), without the softmax. This means the same symbols f and g are used for two different purposes: in SPN, they output logits that are softmaxed to form distributions; in CRF-GNN, they directly define potential functions. While not a mathematical error, this notational reuse is potentially confusing, as it might suggest that f and g play the same role in both models. In reality, in CRF-GNN, f(us) acts as an unnormalized score (like a logit), whereas in SPN, f(us) is explicitly a logit that becomes a probability. The authors could have distinguished these uses, but since the functions are defined differently in different contexts and the parameterizations are clearly separated, context resolves the issue. Still, a careful reader might initially wonder if this implies a hidden connection between the models that isn't there.

comments.title: Inconsistent table column labeling in Table 1
comments.quote:
|**Algorithm**|**PPI-1**
Accuracy
Micro-F1|**PPI-1**
Accuracy
Micro-F1|**PPI-2**
Accuracy
Micro-F1|**PPI-2**
Accuracy
Micro-F1|**PPI-10**
Accuracy
Micro-F1|**PPI-10**
Accuracy
Micro-F1|**PPI**
Accuracy
Micro-F1|**PPI**
Accuracy
Micro-F1|
comments.explanation: At first glance, the header of Table 1 appears to repeat each dataset name (e.g., 'PPI-1') twice with the same metrics ('Accuracy' and 'Micro-F1'), suggesting a possible formatting error or duplicate columns. This is confusing because it implies either redundancy or a misalignment in how results are presented. However, checking the data rows shows that each algorithm has exactly eight values, matching the eight column headers. Moreover, in the results section (5.5), the authors refer to both 'Accuracy' and 'Micro-F1' as separate metrics, which confirms that the table intends to report both metrics per dataset. The real issue is not mathematical but structural: the header formatting is ambiguous due to line breaks, making it seem like each dataset is listed twice rather than once with two metrics. A clearer layout would group 'Accuracy' and 'Micro-F1' under each dataset. While this could mislead a quick reader into thinking results are duplicated, the content itself is consistent — each dataset has two metrics. Still, the presentation risks misinterpretation, especially since other tables (e.g., Table 2) report only Accuracy. This is likely an OCR or LaTeX rendering artifact, but even in the original, the typographic design could cause confusion.

comments.title: Ambiguity in refinement objective compared to formal definition
comments.quote:
In the second step, we update the θ-functions parameterized by the node and edge GNNs to maximize:

Intuitively, we treat the true label ys∗[and][ (]ys∗[, y]t∗[)][ of each node and edge as positive examples, and] encourage the θ-functions to raise up their scores. Meanwhile, those labels sampled from qs(ys) and qst(ys, yt) act as negative examples, and the θ-functions are updated to decrease their scores.
comments.explanation: The passage describes the refinement step as maximizing an objective involving positive and negative examples, but the actual objective function is not written—there is a line break or missing equation after 'maximize:'. From the PAPER SUMMARY, we know the refinement objective should resemble the CRF maximin game: maximizing the expected score of true labels minus the expected score under current marginals. The text description aligns with this: increasing θ-values for true labels (ys*, yst*) and decreasing them for samples from q. However, the absence of the formal equation here — even though it may appear elsewhere in the full paper — makes this part underspecified within the provided context. A reader relying only on this passage cannot reproduce the update rule. While the summary provides the correct form, this section fails to connect clearly to it. Given that refinement is an optional but potentially impactful step, omitting the objective here creates a gap. The intent is clear and consistent with standard contrastive estimation, but the omission affects reproducibility.

comments.title: Node- and edge-GNN sharing variant not fully specified in Table 6
comments.explanation: The variant using a 'shared GNN' for both node and edge marginals is introduced in point 3 of Section 5.5 and presented in Table 6. The results suggest competitive performance, even outperforming the separate-GNN version on PPI-10. However, the method is underspecified: it is unclear how a single GNN is used to produce both node-level and edge-level pseudomarginals. Do the authors share weights in the backbone but use separate heads? Is edge representation derived from paired node outputs? How are edge features handled? While this may be detailed elsewhere, within the provided context, the description is too vague to reproduce. The paper states SPN uses 'a node GNN and an edge GNN' as default, so switching to a shared one is a nontrivial architectural choice. Without clarification on how this sharing is implemented (e.g., parameter sharing scheme, input construction), the comparison lacks methodological transparency, especially since differences in capacity or inductive bias could explain performance shifts.

comments.title: Claim about refinement improvement contradicts Table 4
comments.quote: Only on datasets with sufficient labeled data (e.g., PPI-10, PPI), refinement leads to some improvement.
comments.explanation: This statement is mostly supported: in Table 4, SPN-GAT shows improvement on PPI and PPI-10 when refinement is used, while SPN-GraphSAGE shows improvement on all PPI variants. However, the table shows that for SPN-GAT, refinement increases Micro-F1 on PPI-2 from 71.52 to 71.58, which is a small but non-zero improvement, yet the authors do not highlight this and qualify improvement only for 'PPI-10, PPI'. This is a minor overstatement — technically, there *is* improvement on PPI-2, though small. The concern is resolved upon close reading of the numbers, but the prose dismisses any gain on smaller datasets when one exists. This could mislead readers into thinking refinement universally fails on smaller splits. The authors are correct that gains are larger on PPI-10 and PPI (which have more graphs), so the overall claim is reasonable, but the absolute framing ('only') is too strong given the evidence. A more precise claim would acknowledge marginal gains on intermediate splits.

comments.title: Run time comparison missing SPN variant in Tab 3 header
comments.explanation: In Table 3, the algorithm 'SPN(GAT)' is listed with run times for DBLP and PPI, and it's correctly compared against GAT and CRF(GAT). The notation 'SPN(GAT)' is slightly inconsistent with elsewhere (e.g., 'SPN-GAT' in other tables), but this is likely just stylistic. More importantly, the value 46.86 seconds for SPN(GAT) on DBLP is approximately double that of GAT (23.15), which the text explains by the need to train two GNNs. This makes sense and is consistent. However, the header for the second part of the table (which belongs to Table 4) is pasted adjacent, creating visual confusion as if DBLP and PPI run times include refinement variants. But that part is clearly labeled as Table 4. The real issue is not in the numbers but in the layout: the two tables are merged into one block in the OCR output, which could mislead a reader into thinking SPN(GAT)'s run time depends on refinement. Fortunately, the context clarifies that refinement is optional and not used during training in the default SPN. The numeric values themselves are not problematic — the efficiency claim (SPN much faster than CRF) is strongly supported (500s vs 47s). Layout issues aside, the data and conclusion are sound.

comments.title: Missing clarification on edge-GNN input features
comments.quote: Similarly, we apply the edge GNN to compute a representation vs for each node s, and model τst(ys, yt) as: ... where g is a function mapping a pair of representations to a (|Y| × |Y|)-dimensional logit.
comments.explanation: The paper states that an edge GNN is used to predict edge pseudomarginals τst(ys, yt). However, it says the edge GNN computes a representation vs for each *node* s, not for each edge. This is confusing: if the edge GNN outputs node representations, how are they used for edge-level predictions? The natural assumption is that vs and vt are combined (e.g., concatenated) to form an edge representation, which is then processed by g to yield logits for (ys, yt). But this is not explicitly stated. While this may be standard (e.g., as in GAT or MPNNs), for an edge GNN, one might expect it to operate directly on edge features or pairs. The ambiguity lies in whether 'edge GNN' refers to a GNN that produces edge-aware node embeddings or one that directly processes edges. Given that τst depends on both s and t, and g takes a pair of representations, the implied design is plausible. However, without clarification, a reader might misinterpret the architecture. This is particularly important because the edge GNN’s ability to capture relational context is central to modeling joint dependencies. The omission is minor given context, but it affects full reproducibility.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The provided introduction and sections present a well-structured and technically sound foundation for a research paper. The writing is clear, logical, and effectively situates the proposed "Structured Proxy Network" (SPN) within the existing literature by highlighting the dichotomy between the representation power of GNNs and the collective modeling capabilities of Conditional Random Fields (CRFs). The motivation for the paper is compelling; by identifying the computational bottlenecks and stability issues associated with the maximin optimization of standard CRF-GNN hybrids, the authors establish a clear "gap" that their proxy objective aims to bridge. The most significant strength is this coherent narrative arc—from problem formulation to the proposed methodological shift. To improve the paper, the authors should ensure that the transition from the theoretical "proxy" derivation to the practical implementation is rigorously supported by the subsequent mathematical derivations, as the high-level intuition provided, while intuitive, relies on the assumption that the proxy objective maintains a tight and tractable connection to the original joint distribution likelihood. Beyond the theory, the paper would benefit from a detailed discussion of potential failure cases for the loopy belief propagation inference step, particularly in testing scenarios where distribution shifts between training and test graphs might exacerbate convergence issues.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of fixed-point convergence for loopy belief propagation
comments.explanation: The text erroneously equates a specific parameterization of CRF potentials to a fixed point of standard sum-product loopy belief propagation. This property holds for Tree-Reweighted (TRW) belief propagation but is not generally true for standard loopy BP on arbitrary graphs. The claim lacks the necessary conditions/assumptions regarding graph structure or convergence guarantees.

comments.title: Logical gap in the derivation of the 'near-optimal' joint distribution
comments.explanation: The paper makes strong assertions that matching local pseudomarginals to ground-truth delta functions results in a 'near-optimal' joint distribution. This claim is conceptually underspecified as it ignores the intractable partition function and fails to provide a rigorous link between local KL optimization and global optimality. This concern encompasses the ambiguities surrounding the derivation in paragraph 40 and the moment-matching consistency discussed in paragraphs 36 and 40.

comments.title: Inadequate justification for ignoring consistency constraints
comments.explanation: The authors claim that marginalization consistency constraints can be ignored during optimization because the solution will 'naturally' satisfy them. This is theoretically weak because the node and edge pseudomarginals are parameterized by independent GNNs. Without explicitly enforcing these constraints, there is no guarantee that the independently learned functions will respect the underlying structural dependencies at inference time.

comments.title: Missing formal objective function for model refinement
comments.explanation: The text describes an update rule for refining theta-functions using positive and negative examples but fails to provide the objective function (e.g., the loss formula/gradient expression). The absence of this equation leaves the refinement procedure underspecified for implementation or verification.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in objective divergence measure
comments.explanation: The notation 'I(ys*,yt*)[{ (ys, yt) }]' used for the divergence measure is non-standard and syntactically malformed in the text, making it difficult for the reader to strictly interpret the optimization objective.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The provided introduction and sections present a well-structured and technically sound foundation for a research paper. The writing is clear, logical, and effectively situates the proposed "Structured Proxy Network" (SPN) within the existing literature by highlighting the dichotomy between the representation power of GNNs and the collective modeling capabilities of Conditional Random Fields (CRFs). The motivation for the paper is compelling; by identifying the computational bottlenecks and stability issues associated with the maximin optimization of standard CRF-GNN hybrids, the authors establish a clear "gap" that their proxy objective aims to bridge. The most significant strength is this coherent narrative arc—from problem formulation to the proposed methodological shift. To improve the paper, the authors should ensure that the transition from the theoretical "proxy" derivation to the practical implementation is rigorously supported by the subsequent mathematical derivations, as the high-level intuition provided, while intuitive, relies on the assumption that the proxy objective maintains a tight and tractable connection to the original joint distribution likelihood. Beyond the theory, the paper would benefit from a detailed discussion of potential failure cases for the loopy belief propagation inference step, particularly in testing scenarios where distribution shifts between training and test graphs might exacerbate convergence issues.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of fixed-point convergence for loopy belief propagation
comments.quote: If we parameterize the \theta-functions of p\theta in Eq. (2) in the following way: ... then {\tau_s(y_s)}_{s\in V} and {\tau_{st}(y_s, y_t)}_{(s,t)\in E} are specified by a fixed point of the sum-product loopy belief propagation algorithm
comments.explanation: The text claims that parameterizing the CRF potentials in a specific way as a function of pseudomarginals ensures that these pseudomarginals correspond to a fixed point of the loopy belief propagation algorithm. While this is a known property for Tree-Reweighted (TRW) belief propagation, it is not generally true for standard loopy belief propagation (sum-product) on arbitrary graphs with loops. Sum-product BP is not guaranteed to converge to a fixed point on general graphs, and the relationship between the potentials and fixed-point marginals is highly sensitive to the graph structure. By omitting the specific conditionality (such as tree-reweighted settings) or acknowledging the convergence limitations of sum-product BP, the justification for the proxy objective lacks rigor.

comments.title: Typographical error in moment-matching condition claim
comments.quote: and pθ( ys, yt | xV , E ) ≈ I ys∗ {ys} . We see that the moment-matching
comments.explanation: In the paragraph following Equation (9), the authors discuss the moment-matching conditions. They correctly write the condition for node marginals as pθ(ys) ≈ I_ys*{ys}. However, for the edge marginals, they write pθ(ys, yt) ≈ I_ys*{ys}. This is mathematically inconsistent with the definition of edge potential moment matching provided in Equation (4), which defines the condition as pθ(ys, yt) = I(ys*, yt*). The typo restricts the condition to only the first node's label, which is logically incomplete in the context of edge-pair dependencies.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'optimal joint distribution' derivation
comments.quote: Combining these two sets of formula results inconditions in Eq. (4) for the optimal joint label distribution are roughly achieved, implying that the pθ( ys ) ≈ I ys∗ {ys} and pθ( ys, yt ) ≈ I ys∗ {ys} .
comments.explanation: The OCR-damaged phrase 'results inconditions' is illegible. While I can infer the authors meant 'results in the conditions' or 'results in fulfilling the conditions', the sentence structure is broken. Furthermore, the subsequent claim about the joint distribution being a 'near-optimal solution' lacks direct justification within the text. While they cite Wainwright & Jordan (2008) regarding the proxy, the link between specifically minimizing the KL divergence of local pseudomarginals and the global joint distribution optimality is asserted rather than demonstrated, which is a conceptual leap for the reader.

comments.title: Formula index inconsistency in Proposition 1 summation
comments.quote: ∑ ys τst ( ys, yt ) = τt ( yt )
comments.explanation: In the summation constraint of Proposition 1, the LaTeX markup appears inconsistent with standard marginalization notation. The index of the summation is 'ys', and the function input is 'ys, yt'. The resulting marginal should yield 'τt(yt)'. While this is standard for marginalizing out the first variable, the OCR identifies an odd character '' and the variable mapping across the sum seems to suggest a specific index alignment that is slightly confusing to parse, though likely correct in intent.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in the gradient update objective
comments.quote:
In the second step, we update the θ-functions parameterized by the node and edge GNNs to maximize:

Intuitively, we treat the true label ys* and ( ys*, yt* ) of each node and edge as positive examples, and encourage the θ-functions to raise up their scores. Meanwhile, those labels sampled from qs( ys ) and qst( ys, yt ) act as negative examples, and the θ-functions are updated to decrease their scores.
comments.explanation: The text describes an update rule for θ-functions (treating true labels as positive and sampled marginals as negative), but the actual mathematical expression for this objective is missing from the provided passage. The text cuts off after 'maximize:' and fails to provide the objective function (e.g., a contrastive loss or log-likelihood gradient). While it mentions that these terms act as positive/negative examples, without the formal equation for this specific objective in the refinement step, it is underspecified how the gradient is actually calculated to ensure convergence toward the maximin solution.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of the proxy objective's consistency constraints
comments.quote: The last consistency constraint (i.e. \sum_{yt} \tau_{st}(y_s, y_t) = \tau_t(y_t) and \sum_{ys} \tau_{st}(y_s, y_t) = \tau_s(y_s)) can be ignored during optimization, since by optimizing the objective function, the optimal pseudomarginals \tau should well approximate the observed node and edge marginals... and hence \tau will almost naturally satisfy the consistency constraint.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the consistency constraints can be ignored. While it is true that minimizing KL divergence to the empirical distributions (delta functions on true labels) forces the pseudomarginals to be consistent with the ground truth, the claim that they 'almost naturally' satisfy the consistency constraint in a way that respects the CRF structural dependencies is optimistic. If the GNNs for nodes and edges are optimized independently (as Eq. 10 suggests), they may converge to the empirical distribution at training nodes, but their interaction at test-time inference (via the fixed point of belief propagation) may be unstable if they do not explicitly satisfy the marginalization constraints. The authors admit they tried constrained optimization without 'improvement', but the justification for ignoring the theoretical requirement for consistency is weak.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in constraint definition
comments.quote: consider a set of nonzero pseudomarginals {τs(ys)}s∈V and {τst(ys, yt)}(st)∈E which satisfy ∑ysτst(ys, yt) = τt(yt) and ∑ytτst(ys, yt) = τs(ys) for all (s, t) ∈ E.
comments.explanation: In Proposition 1, the summation indices in the consistency constraints are swapped relative to the targets. progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
### High-Level Feedback

This paper, published at ICLR 2022, presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to inductive node classification by combining the strengths of graph neural networks (GNNs) and conditional random fields (CRFs). The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, with a clear articulation of the limitations of existing GNN-based and CRF-based methods, and a logical progression to the proposed Structured Proxy Network (SPN). However, the paper's most significant issue lies in the **justification and evaluation of the approximation** used to circumvent the challenging maximin optimization. The authors draw on theoretical connections between joint and marginal distributions in Markov networks to propose a proxy objective, but the manuscript would benefit from a more precise characterization of the conditions under which this approximation is near-optimal and how errors from this approximation propagate to downstream classification performance. Furthermore, while the experimental results demonstrate superiority over a range of baselines, the paper would be strengthened by a more thorough ablation study isolating the impact of the proxy optimization from the architectural benefits of the GNN-based potentials, as well as a discussion of scenarios (if any) where the approximation may break down. Overall, the paper is of high quality and represents a meaningful contribution, but a deeper theoretical and empirical analysis of the proposed proxy's fidelity would significantly enhance its impact and rigor.

comments.title: Incorrect indicator function in the second approximation for edge marginals
comments.quote: Combining these two sets of formula results in conditions in Eq. (4) for the optimal joint label distribution are roughly achieved, implying that the pθ(ys) ≈ I_{ys∗}{ys} and pθ(ys,yt) ≈ I_{ys∗}{ys}.
comments.explanation: The sentence states that after combining the approximations, we have pθ(ys,yt) ≈ I_{ys∗}{ys}. This cannot be correct: the edge marginal approximation should involve an indicator on the pair (y_s^*, y_t^*) over (y_s, y_t), not a single node indicator. The context clearly intends I_{(y_s^*, y_t^*)}{(y_s, y_t)} (as used earlier in the paper in Eq. 4). The error is likely a typographical mistake (possibly introduced during OCR or editing) and would confuse readers about the moment‑matching conditions. The discrepancy is substantive because it misrepresents the equality that defines optimal θ‑functions.

comments.title: Potential missing formula or typo in refinement objective
comments.quote: In the second step, we update the \theta-functions parameterized by the node and edge GNNs to maximize:
comments.explanation: The passage states that the second step of refinement updates the θ-functions to maximize an expression, but the actual expression is missing — the sentence ends abruptly with a colon and then moves to an intuitive description. While the intuitive description (treat true labels as positive examples, sampled labels from q as negative examples) suggests the objective might be something like E_q[log p_θ] or a contrastive term, the precise mathematical form is not given. Given that the passage is extracted via OCR, it is possible that the expression was present in the original (e.g., an equation number or inline math) but was dropped or corrupted during extraction. Without it, a reader cannot know exactly what loss is being maximized, which is a key algorithmic detail. This could affect reproducibility. However, the surrounding context of the maximin game (Eq. 3) and the intuitive description provide some guidance. I note the authors likely included the expression in the original paper; the issue here is likely an OCR truncation. But as it stands, the passage is underspecified for the refinement step.

comments.title: Inconsistent proxy-problem Micro-F1 value for PPI-10 in Table 5
comments.quote: We show the results for optimizing SPN-GAT in Tab. 5. ... | Proxy Problem | **58.78**±_1.21 | **49.02**±_0.78 | **95.87**±_0.02
comments.explanation: The passage states that Table 5 compares learning methods for SPN-GAT. In Table 1, the Micro-F1 for SPN-GAT on PPI-10 is 94.41±0.21 (without refinement, as seen in Table 4). Table 5 lists the proxy problem value for PPI-10 as 95.87±0.02, which is actually the Micro-F1 achieved by SPN-GCNII (Table 1) or the shared-GNN variant (Table 6). This discrepancy suggests either the table entry is mislabeled (it should be 94.41 for SPN-GAT) or the text incorrectly claims the backbone is SPN-GAT. Such an inconsistency undermines the comparison of learning methods because a reader might erroneously attribute the higher value to the proxy problem with a GAT backbone, exaggerating the improvement over pseudolikelihood (GMNN) and maximin game (CRF-GAT).

comments.title: Ambiguous use of edge GNN as standalone node predictor in case study
comments.quote: the edge GNN (middle column) also makes a mistake in the bottom case. Finally, by combining GAT and edge GNN with a CRF, the SPN (right column) is able to predict the correct labels for all nodes.
comments.explanation: In the SPN framework, the edge GNN parameterizes edge pseudomarginals τ_st(y_s, y_t), not direct node label predictions. The passage presents the edge GNN as if it produces node-level predictions comparable to GAT and SPN. This is ambiguous because it is not explained how node labels are derived from the edge GNN alone (e.g., by marginalizing over neighbors, using some heuristic, or treating it as a separate classifier). Without clarification, a careful reader may incorrectly infer that the edge GNN is intended to perform node classification on its own, which is not its role in the SPN model. The comparison in the case study is therefore methodologically unclear and could misrepresent the contribution of the edge potentials.

comments.title: Imprecise reference to CRFs in convergence analysis
comments.quote: CRFs and SPNs w/o proxy suffer from high variance and low accuracy. In contrast, owing to the near-optimal joint distribution found by solving the proxy problem, SPNs get much higher accuracy with lower variance even without refinement (see initial results of SPNs at epoch 0).
comments.explanation: The term 'CRFs' here is ambiguous: it could refer to traditional linear-parameter CRFs (low capacity) or to GNN-parameterized CRFs (high capacity). Earlier in the paper, CRFs are typically associated with log-linear potentials, while SPNs w/o proxy are explicitly GNN-parameterized CRFs trained via the maximin game. LumpiNg them together obscures that the two baselines differ fundamentally in model capacity. The claim that both 'suffer from high variance and low accuracy' conflates distinct sources of difficulty (low capacity vs. unstable optimization). A reader may not be sure which baseline is being compared. While this is a minor framing concern, it could mislead interpretations of why the proxy problem helps.
